transcript:
Right now you're looking at a heart-shaped swarm slowly climbing out of near-total darkness — pale blue static-like flecks scattered through a haze of dim red circles, threaded together by faint purple lines. Nothing new gets added as it brightens. It's all just... becoming visible.

That's the trick, and it's literal in the code. Every one of those 850 elements — look in the section labeled "nervous structures" — gets created and appended to the DOM with opacity set to zero, before anything animates. Then a function called "reveal what was always there there" — no exaggeration, that's its actual name — slowly ramps opacity up over about fourteen seconds using an exponential curve. Nothing is born. Everything was already sitting in the document, just invisible.

And the two things you eventually see pulsing — the nervous shimmer, that fast, jittery blue static, and the blood rhythm, the slower red circles that drift and breathe — are named directly after Cage's two inescapable sounds in the anechoic chamber. The piece isn't simulating silence and breaking it. It's showing you that the "silence" already contained a structure — you just couldn't see it yet.

animates. Then a function called "reveal what was always there there" — no exaggeration, that's
see pulsing — the nervous shimmer, that fast, jittery blue static,
the slower red circles that drift and breathe — are named
